description: Primary-publication abstracts for the 5 SURPASS Phase 3 trials of tirzepatide in T2D, retrieved via PubMed MCP get_article_metadata on 2026-05-13. All 5 PMIDs verified vs PubMed records (no PMID guessing). Source: PubMed (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/). DOI links included per PubMed attribution requirement.
retrieval_method: mcp__claude_ai_PubMed__get_article_metadata batch lookup; PMIDs cross-checked against the published trial-NCT links section of each article.

publications.title: Efficacy and safety of a novel dual GIP and GLP-1 receptor agonist tirzepatide in patients with type 2 diabetes (SURPASS-1): a double-blind, randomised, phase 3 trial.
publications.abstract: Despite advancements in care, many people with type 2 diabetes do not meet treatment goals; thus, development of new therapies is needed. We aimed to assess efficacy, safety, and tolerability of novel dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and GLP-1 receptor agonist tirzepatide monotherapy versus placebo in people with type 2 diabetes inadequately controlled by diet and exercise alone. We did a 40-week, double-blind, randomised, placebo-controlled, phase 3 trial (SURPASS-1), at 52 medical research centres and hospitals in India, Japan, Mexico, and the USA. Adult participants (>=18 years) were included if they had type 2 diabetes inadequately controlled by diet and exercise alone and if they were naive to injectable diabetes therapy. Participants were randomly assigned (1:1:1:1) via computer-generated random sequence to once a week tirzepatide (5, 10, or 15 mg), or placebo. All participants, investigators, and the sponsor were masked to treatment assignment. The primary endpoint was the mean change in glycated haemoglobin (HbA1c) from baseline at 40 weeks. From June 3, 2019, to Oct 28, 2020, of 705 individuals assessed for eligibility, 478 (mean baseline HbA1c 7.9% [63 mmol/mol], age 54.1 years, 231 [48%] women) were randomly assigned to tirzepatide 5 mg (n=121), 10 mg (n=121), 15 mg (n=121), or placebo (n=115). At 40 weeks, all tirzepatide doses were superior to placebo for changes from baseline in HbA1c. Mean HbA1c decreased from baseline by 1.87% with tirzepatide 5 mg, 1.89% with 10 mg, and 2.07% with 15 mg versus +0.04% with placebo, resulting in estimated treatment differences versus placebo of -1.91%, -1.93%, and -2.11% (all p<0.0001). Tirzepatide induced a dose-dependent bodyweight loss ranging from 7.0 to 9.5 kg. The most frequent adverse events were mild to moderate gastrointestinal events. No clinically significant or severe hypoglycaemia were reported with tirzepatide. Tirzepatide showed robust improvements in glycaemic control and bodyweight, without increased risk of hypoglycaemia. Funding: Eli Lilly and Company.
publications.primary_endpoint_text: Tirzepatide produced HbA1c reductions of 1.87, 1.89, and 2.07 percentage points (5, 10, 15 mg) vs +0.04 with placebo at week 40 (all p<0.0001); dose-response approximately monotonic across the three doses.

publications.title: Tirzepatide versus Semaglutide Once Weekly in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes.
publications.abstract: Tirzepatide is a dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist that is under development for the treatment of type 2 diabetes. The efficacy and safety of once-weekly tirzepatide as compared with semaglutide, a selective GLP-1 receptor agonist, are unknown. In an open-label, 40-week, phase 3 trial, we randomly assigned 1879 patients, in a 1:1:1:1 ratio, to receive tirzepatide at a dose of 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg or semaglutide at a dose of 1 mg. At baseline, the mean glycated hemoglobin level was 8.28%, the mean age 56.6 years, and the mean weight 93.7 kg. The primary end point was the change in the glycated hemoglobin level from baseline to 40 weeks. The estimated mean change from baseline in the glycated hemoglobin level was -2.01 percentage points, -2.24 percentage points, and -2.30 percentage points with 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg of tirzepatide, respectively, and -1.86 percentage points with semaglutide; the estimated differences between the 5-mg, 10-mg, and 15-mg tirzepatide groups and the semaglutide group were -0.15 percentage points (95% CI -0.28 to -0.03; P=0.02), -0.39 percentage points (95% CI -0.51 to -0.26; P<0.001), and -0.45 percentage points (95% CI -0.57 to -0.32; P<0.001), respectively. Tirzepatide at all doses was noninferior and superior to semaglutide. Reductions in body weight were greater with tirzepatide than with semaglutide. The most common adverse events were gastrointestinal and were primarily mild to moderate in severity. In patients with type 2 diabetes, tirzepatide was noninferior and superior to semaglutide with respect to the mean change in the glycated hemoglobin level from baseline to 40 weeks. (Funded by Eli Lilly; SURPASS-2 ClinicalTrials.gov number, NCT03987919.).
publications.primary_endpoint_text: Tirzepatide 5, 10, 15 mg produced HbA1c reductions of -2.01, -2.24, -2.30 (NEJM efficacy estimand) or -2.09, -2.37, -2.46 (AACT registry treatment-regimen estimand) vs -1.86 with semaglutide 1 mg at week 40; noninferior and superior to semaglutide at all 3 doses (P<=0.02).

publications.title: Once-weekly tirzepatide versus once-daily insulin degludec as add-on to metformin with or without SGLT2 inhibitors in patients with type 2 diabetes (SURPASS-3): a randomised, open-label, parallel-group, phase 3 trial.
publications.abstract: Tirzepatide is a novel dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and GLP-1 receptor agonist under development for the treatment of type 2 diabetes. We aimed to assess efficacy and safety of tirzepatide versus titrated insulin degludec in people with type 2 diabetes inadequately controlled by metformin with or without SGLT2 inhibitors. In this open-label, parallel-group, multicentre (122 sites), multinational (13 countries), phase 3 study, eligible participants (>=18 years) had baseline HbA1c 7.0-10.5%, BMI >=25 kg/m2, were insulin-naive and on metformin alone or with an SGLT2 inhibitor for >=3 months. Participants were randomly assigned 1:1:1:1 to once-weekly SC tirzepatide (5, 10, or 15 mg) or once-daily SC titrated insulin degludec for 52 weeks. The primary efficacy endpoint was non-inferiority of tirzepatide 10 mg or 15 mg, or both, versus insulin degludec in mean change from baseline in HbA1c at week 52. Between April 1 and Nov 15, 2019, 1444 participants were randomly assigned. The mITT population was 1437 (tirz 5 mg n=358; 10 mg n=360; 15 mg n=359; degludec n=360). From a mean baseline HbA1c of 8.17%, the reductions at week 52 were 1.93% (SE 0.05) for tirz 5 mg, 2.20% (0.05) for 10 mg, and 2.37% (0.05) for 15 mg, vs 1.34% (0.05) for insulin degludec. The non-inferiority margin of 0.3% was met. The estimated treatment difference versus insulin degludec ranged from -0.59% to -1.04% (p<0.0001 for all). All three tirzepatide doses also decreased bodyweight (-7.5 to -12.9 kg) while degludec increased bodyweight by 2.3 kg. Hypoglycaemia (<54 mg/dL or severe) was rarer with tirzepatide (1-2%) than degludec (7%). Tirzepatide showed a similar safety profile to GLP-1 receptor agonists. Funding: Eli Lilly and Company.
publications.primary_endpoint_text: Tirzepatide 5, 10, 15 mg produced HbA1c reductions of 1.93, 2.20, 2.37 percentage points vs 1.34 with insulin degludec at week 52 (all p<0.0001); monotonic dose-response, non-inferiority margin 0.3% met for both primary doses.

publications.title: Tirzepatide versus insulin glargine in type 2 diabetes and increased cardiovascular risk (SURPASS-4): a randomised, open-label, parallel-group, multicentre, phase 3 trial.
publications.abstract: We aimed to assess efficacy and safety, with a special focus on cardiovascular safety, of the novel dual GIP and GLP-1 receptor agonist tirzepatide versus insulin glargine in adults with type 2 diabetes and high cardiovascular risk inadequately controlled on oral glucose-lowering medications. This open-label, parallel-group, phase 3 study was done in 187 sites in 14 countries. Eligible participants (>=18 years) had T2D on any combination of metformin, sulfonylurea, or SGLT2 inhibitor, baseline HbA1c 7.5-10.5%, BMI >=25 kg/m2, and established CVD or high CV risk. Participants were randomly assigned 1:1:1:3 to SC tirzepatide (5, 10, or 15 mg once weekly) or insulin glargine 100 U/mL (titrated to fasting blood glucose <100 mg/dL). The primary endpoint was non-inferiority (0.3% boundary) of tirzepatide 10 or 15 mg, or both, versus glargine in HbA1c change from baseline to 52 weeks. Patients were recruited between Nov 20, 2018 and Dec 30, 2019; 2002 randomized, 1995 in mITT (tirz 5/10/15 mg n=329/328/338; glargine n=1000). At 52 weeks, mean HbA1c changes were -2.43% (SE 0.05) for 10 mg and -2.58% (0.05) for 15 mg, vs -1.44% (0.03) for glargine. Estimated treatment difference vs glargine -0.99% (multiplicity-adjusted 97.5% CI -1.13 to -0.86) for 10 mg and -1.14% (-1.28 to -1.00) for 15 mg; non-inferiority met at both doses. Hypoglycaemia (<54 mg/dL or severe) was lower with tirzepatide (6-9%) vs glargine (19%). Adjudicated MACE-4 events occurred in 109 participants; HR vs glargine 0.74 (95% CI 0.51-1.08). Tirzepatide treatment was not associated with excess cardiovascular risk. Funding: Eli Lilly and Company.
publications.primary_endpoint_text: Tirzepatide 10, 15 mg produced HbA1c reductions of -2.43, -2.58 (5 mg from key secondary: -2.24) percentage points vs -1.44 with insulin glargine at week 52; monotonic dose-response; lower hypoglycaemia vs glargine and no excess CV risk (MACE-4 HR 0.74).

publications.title: Effect of Subcutaneous Tirzepatide vs Placebo Added to Titrated Insulin Glargine on Glycemic Control in Patients With Type 2 Diabetes: The SURPASS-5 Randomized Clinical Trial.
publications.abstract: The effects of tirzepatide, a dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist, as an addition to insulin glargine for treatment of type 2 diabetes have not been described. To assess the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide added to insulin glargine in patients with type 2 diabetes with inadequate glycemic control. Randomized phase 3 clinical trial conducted at 45 medical research centers and hospitals in 8 countries (enrollment from August 30, 2019, to March 20, 2020; follow-up completed January 13, 2021) in 475 adults with T2D and inadequate glycemic control while treated with once-daily insulin glargine with or without metformin. Patients were randomized 1:1:1:1 to once-weekly SC injections of 5 mg (n=116), 10 mg (n=119), or 15 mg (n=120) tirzepatide or volume-matched placebo (n=120) over 40 weeks. Tirzepatide was initiated at 2.5 mg/week and escalated by 2.5 mg every 4 weeks until the assigned dose was achieved. The primary end point was mean change from baseline in HbA1c at week 40. Among 475 randomized participants (211 [44%] women; mean age 60.6 years; mean HbA1c 8.31%), 451 (94.9%) completed the trial. At week 40, mean HbA1c change from baseline was -2.40% with 10 mg tirzepatide and -2.34% with 15 mg tirzepatide vs -0.86% with placebo (10 mg: difference vs placebo, -1.53% [97.5% CI -1.80 to -1.27]; 15 mg: difference, -1.47% [97.5% CI -1.75 to -1.20]; P<0.001 for both). Mean HbA1c change was -2.11% with 5 mg tirzepatide (difference vs placebo, -1.24% [95% CI -1.48 to -1.01]; P<0.001). Mean body weight change was -5.4 kg with 5 mg, -7.5 kg with 10 mg, -8.8 kg with 15 mg, and +1.6 kg with placebo. Higher percentages of tirzepatide-treated patients had HbA1c <7% (85%-90% vs 34% placebo). Among patients with T2D and inadequate glycemic control despite insulin glargine, the addition of subcutaneous tirzepatide vs placebo resulted in statistically significant improvements in glycemic control after 40 weeks.
publications.primary_endpoint_text: Tirzepatide 5, 10, 15 mg produced HbA1c reductions of -2.11 (key secondary), -2.40, -2.34 (NEJM/JAMA efficacy estimand) or -2.23, -2.59, -2.59 (AACT registry treatment-regimen estimand) percentage points vs -0.86 (abstract) / -0.93 (AACT) with placebo at week 40 added to glargine background; all p<0.001 vs placebo.
